data.title: Wall Street ends lower as weak jobs growth fans recession worries
data.description:
Key points:
  • Wall Street indexes end lower, with Dow leading declines
  • Energy leads S&P 500 sector declines; real estate rises the most
  • Dollar falls ~0.5%, gold up >1%, U.S. crude dips >2%, bitcoin up ~1%
  • US 10-yr Treasury yield falls to ~4.09%, hits lowest level since April

WALL STREET ENDS LOWER AS WEAK JOBS GROWTH FANS RECESSION WORRI diIES

Wall Street ended the week on a sour note, with the S&P 500 losing ground as investors grew more worried about a stumbling economy after data showed job growth weakened sharply in August.

The unemployment rate increased to nearly a four-year high of 4.3% in August, confirming that labor market conditions were softening and sealing the case for a Federal Reserve interest rate cut later this month.

Friday's report reflects a trend of slowing job growth since April, with economists blaming President Donald Trump's policies, mainly tariffs on imports, an immigration crackdown and mass firings of public workers.

"Today’s softer than expected jobs report underlines the growing downside risks to the labor market. Hiring is running close to stall speed, and the breadth of jobs gains remains poor," said S imon Dangoor, head of fixed income macro strategies, Goldman Sachs Asset Management in New York.

data.title: Treasury Secretary Bessent calls for entire review of Fed
data.description:
Key points:
  • Bessent criticizes Fed's "missteps," calls for nonpartisan review
  • Interviews for new Fed chair started Friday
  • Trump seeks to remake Fed board

U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on Friday called for renewed scrutiny of the Federal Reserve, including its power to set interest rates, as the Trump administration intensifies its efforts to exert control over a central bank whose insulation from short-term political pressures is widely seen as key to effective control of inflation.

"There must also be an honest, independent, nonpartisan review of the entire institution, including monetary policy, regulation, communications, staffing and research," Bessent wrote in the Wall Street Journal, also calling for the Fed to leave bank supervision to other governmental authorities and to "scale back the distortions it causes in the economy," including by bond purchases made outside of true crisis conditions.

"The Fed must change course," he said in a longer piece published in The International Economy that excoriated the Fed for policies he said helped feed inflation, enriched the rich at the expense of the poor, and exacerbated the housing affordability crisis.

He also accused the central bank of partisanship and misusing public funds for everything from a renovation of its headquarters to its police force, amplifying charges made by Fed critics within and outside of the Trump administration that the Fed has argued don't reflect the facts.

Bessent's barrage of criticism - which he characterized as a bid to restore the Fed's independence - came as he began a series of interviews on Friday in search of candidates to replace Fed Chair Jerome Powell, whose term ends in May, a source familiar with the process said. It was not immediately clear who would be interviewed.

President Donald Trump has been unhappy with Powell since shortly after making him Fed chair in 2018, and all this year has ratcheted up the pressure on the Fed to lower rates and has moved to install allies at the central bank who will push for cuts. Last week he said he was removing Fed Governor Lisa Cook over allegations of mortgage fraud, which Cook says are unfounded; Cook is suing to stop the firing and remains in her job for now.

Trump's nominee to fill an open seat at the Fed, Stephen Miran, said at his nomination hearing this week that he'll take unpaid leave from his job as White House economic advisor while he does the Fed job, a situation that Democrats say impugns his ability to make monetary policy decisions independently of the president.

The White House has previously indicated that the list of potential Powell successors includes National Economic Council director Kevin Hassett and former Fed Governor Kevin Warsh along with current Fed Governor Christopher Waller.

Some of Bessent's criticisms echo those of the Fed chair candidates; Warsh, for instance, has long called for "regime change" at the Fed.

The Fed's Waller was one of two Fed governors to dissent against the Fed's decision in July not to cut interest rates.

The Fed does look set to kick off a series of reductions this month to shore up an increasingly fragile labor market, though no current policymakers have signaled support for the kind of deep rate cuts that Trump has demanded or the wholesale remaking of the Fed that Bessent is now advocating.

data.title: Trump Gives Defense Department a New Name. Why It Matters. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

The U.S. military establishment has a new name, and investors should take note of it.

President Donald Trump will sign an executive order that "authorizes the Secretary of Defense, the Department of Defense, and subordinate officials to use secondary titles such as 'Secretary of War,' 'Department of War,' and 'Deputy Secretary of War' in official correspondence, public communications, ceremonial contexts, and non-statutory documents within the executive branch," according to a draft of the order reviewed by Barron's.

The U.S. military establishment has been referred to as the Department of Defense since the 1940s. An official name change would require an act of Congress, but using secondary titles gets around that issue.

The president believes the new name projects "strength and resolve." The order adds: "The United States military is the strongest and most lethal fighting force in the world [and] should have a name that reflects its unmatched power and readiness."

It probably is an afterthought for defense investors, though. "It's an aberration compared to the way the rest of the world names their ministries," says Capital Alpha Partners analyst Byron Callan. "It's not going to deter anybody."

Defense stocks have performed relatively well early in Trump's second term. His focus on unmanned technology, missile defense, and increased spending by allies has helped. Coming into Friday trading, the iShares U.S. Aerospace and Defense exchange-traded fund was up 36% since the Nov. election, about 23 percentage points ahead of the S&P 500. Among major defense contractors, only Lockheed Martin shares are down over that span, with the company facing some headwinds for its F-35 fighter jet program.

The ETF dropped 0.2% on Friday, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 0.3% and 0.5%, respectively. Lockheed stock rose 1.6%, closing at $463.90.

The name changes come after China hosted a military parade on Sept. 3 in Beijing as a show of strength and resolve. The event was attended by Russia's Vladimir Putin and North Korea's Kim Jong Un.

China unveiled some of its new military technology, including advanced tanks, drones, antidrone lasers, stealth fighter jets, and autonomous naval vessels, among other systems.

The U.S. has similar systems and leading military technology. "One [parade] nuance is that China is focused more on multi-domain, joint operations," added Callan. That's an approach pioneered by the U.S. that integrates land, sea, air, and cyber assets. "They have learned well."

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

data.title: OPEC+ meets this weekend at a precarious time for crude oil. Here's what's at stake.
data.description:
By Myra P. Saefong

Some OPEC+ members will consider further hikes to crude-output quotas this weekend

Oil prices posted a sharp weekly decline ahead of Sunday's OPEC+ meeting.

After major oil producers earlier this year decided to lift their output quotas, prices dropped to a four-year low, then recovered those losses to post three straight months of gains.

The market now, however, has a lot more to contend with as OPEC+ considers a second round of production hikes at a meeting this weekend, against a backdrop of dismal U.S. jobs data and geopolitical risk that's dimmed prospects for demand and boosted potential for a supply surplus this year and next.

Expectations for a further output increase this weekend by OPEC+ - the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries and its allies - contributed to a more than 3% weekly drop in U.S. benchmark oil prices.

Friday's selloff was "primarily a bearish supply narrative - headlines about OPEC+ adding barrels are emboldening shorts - while a weak risk backdrop is keeping would-be dip buyers on the sidelines," said Rebecca Babin, senior energy trader and managing director at CIBC Private Wealth.

Data on Friday showed that the U.S. unemployment rate rose to a nearly four-year high and the market continues to deal with ongoing political uncertainty surrounding President Donald Trump's global tariffs, dulling expectations for energy demand.

"It's a bit of both supply and macro, but supply is doing the heavy lifting" when it comes to the decline in oil prices, Babin told MarketWatch.

At a meeting planned for Sunday, eight members of OPEC+ that are sometimes referred to as the "V8," or "voluntary eight," are expected to consider gradually unwinding 1.66 million barrels per day in production cuts that began in April 2023.

That would come at a precarious time for the oil market, as the V8 would have fully unwound last year's production cuts of 2.2 million bpd by the end of this month. The V8 began increasing their quotas in April, then accelerated the return of those barrels to the group's production quotas through September.

Oil prices fell as many had expected in the wake of that decision. U.S. benchmark West Texas Intermediate crude (CL00) (CL.1) settled at $57.13 on May 5, its lowest since February 2021, then rebounded to post gains in May, June and July, before an August decline.

Markets were able to "adequately digest the reintroduction of OPEC barrels" so far this year, given that those barrels were reintroduced during a period of relatively tight crude inventories, said Kenny Zhu, assistant vice president of energy and commodities research at Global X. "Economic resilience among Western nations, as well as opportunistic buying among Chinese refiners, further buoyed consumption at current levels."

First round of quota hikes

Oil prices have declined so far this year, with U.S. benchmark prices down 14%, but they've held relatively well above $60 a barrel despite forecasts for steeper declines to the $40 level.

Shortly after the V8 announced a much-larger-than-expected production-quota increase of 411,000 barrels per day for May, U.S. oil prices marked their lowest finish since 2021.

Read archived story: Oil prices are cratering on Trump's tariffs. So why does OPEC+ think the world needs more crude?

The V8's decision was particularly surprising in the wake of Trump's plans to implement global tariffs, which raised the risk of a widespread economic slowdown that could weaken energy demand.

Still, oil prices managed to rebound from four-year lows thanks, in part, to seasonal strength in oil demand and limits to output capacity.

Read: Can OPEC+ flood the world with crude? It's harder than oil traders think.

As the group began unwinding those cuts, much of the oil was already in the market because of overproduction by Iraq, Kazakhstan and Russia, said Anas Alhajji, an independent energy expert and managing partner at Energy Outlook Advisors. So in the beginning, the increase in the production ceiling "legitimized" that overproduction, he said.

The announced increases were also "production ceilings" or quotas, Alhajji said, not actual production gains. Many counted "production-ceiling increases as net global supply, which was inaccurate."

Production differs from supply, he noted: "Global oil markets depend on the group's exports, not production."

Analysts, meanwhile, also overlooked rising domestic oil demand in those producing countries, which saw higher fuel consumption during the summer months, he added.

A chart showing OPEC+ oil exports provided by Kpler shows a decline in the group's exports from June through August.

Matt Smith, lead U.S. analyst at Kpler, told MarketWatch that Middle East crude exports tend to be lower during the summer because of "direct crude burn," which redirects barrels away from exports and to domestic use. "This has been particularly prominent in Saudi Arabia this summer," he said, adding that Kpler believes the Saudis' direct crude burn has climbed to a record in recent months.

OPEC+ has also "not been able to restore the full commitment of an increase in its production," said Gaurav Sharma, associate director of consulting and corporates at Acuity Knowledge Partners. The increase, meanwhile, has mainly come from Saudi Arabia, while Iran and Iraq's production has declined, he said. The Saudis are known to have the largest spare crude-oil production capacity in OPEC+.

Round 2

A second round of production hikes, however, could change the narrative for the oil market.

If OPEC+ proceeds with unwinding the 1.66 million bpd in quota cuts, that would likely "signal the potential for larger surpluses" in the fourth quarter, on top of the already anticipated surplus of more than 1 million bpd, said CIBC's Babin. In its latest monthly report, the International Energy Agency forecast 2025 world oil demand at 103.7 million bpd and supply of 105.5 million bpd.

The potential for larger surpluses could further weigh on prices, said Babin. However, it's important to note that the actual increases will almost certainly "fall short of the headline numbers, as some members are already at capacity, others are compensating for past overproduction, and some may keep barrels at home to meet domestic demand," she said.

If instead the group pauses the unwind, Babin said she expects a "relief rally," particularly since WTI positioning is very short and that could spark a round of short covering.

She also admits that a "surprise outright cut" is also possible, but probability is "very low." OPEC+ has stressed its flexibility, but has also shown "less willingness to cut production to support prices after that strategy failed to deliver the outcomes they hoped for."

Either way, the upcoming OPEC+ meet is a "key event because it will directly shape how analysts adjust balances into year-end," said Babin. Still, it's far from the only factor that will drive crude.

'In the near term, OPEC is clearly in the driver's seat, but it's not the only game in town for oil prices.'Rebecca Babin, CIBC Private Wealth

"Progress - or lack thereof - on a Russian-Ukrainian cease-fire could shift sanctions and supply estimates," she noted. Global economic data will "feed into demand outlooks and sentiment, and tariff headlines could swing demand expectations higher or lower."

"In the near term, OPEC is clearly in the driver's seat, but it's not the only game in town for oil prices," Babin said.

-Myra P. Saefong

East West Petroleum Shareholders Approve Capital Reduction and All Other Matters at the Annual and Special Meeting
data.description:
Vancouver, British Columbia--(Newsfile Corp. - September 5, 2025) - East West Petroleum Corp. ("East West" or the "Company") is pleased to announce that at the Annual and Special Meeting of Shareholders of the Company (the "Meeting") held on September 5, 2025, the shareholders re-elected Nick DeMare, Mark T. Brown and Kevin William Haney to the board of directors (the "Board"). Giacomo Grassi did not stand for re-election at the Meeting.

The shareholders passed all resolutions including (i) an ordinary resolution to ratify the Company's existing stock option plan, pursuant to which the Company may grant stock options up to 10% of its issued and outstanding common shares at the time of the grant; and (ii) a special resolution to approve the return of capital by way of a reduction in stated capital of the Company (the "Capital Reduction"). In accordance with the special resolution passed by the shareholders, the Board has determined that the stated capital of the Company, which is currently $37,224,841, will be reduced by up to $3 million, pursuant to the Business Corporations Act (British Columbia), for the purposes of distributing to the holders of common shares of the Company a portion of the Company's cash in the amount of $0.03 per common share (the "Distribution").

The Company will issue a further news release once the effective date for the Capital Reduction and the Distribution is finalized.

Further details in respect of the Capital Reduction and Distribution are available in the management information circular of the Company in respect of the Meeting held on September 5, 2025, a copy of which is available free of charge under the Company's profile at the Canadian Securities Administrators' website at www.sedarplus.ca.

Following the Meeting, the Board appointed Nick DeMare as Interim CEO and Corporate Secretary of the Company and Harvey Lim as Interim CFO. Messrs. DeMare, Brown, and Haney were appointed to the audit committee.

data.title: A10 Networks Names Michelle Caron as Finance Chief
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

A10 Networks named Michelle Caron as chief financial officer.

Brian Becker, who has served in the role since 2020, is stepping down, the San Jose, Calif., network company said Friday. The transition is scheduled for Sept. 24.

Caron, 58, most recently served as chief financial officer of Beckman Coulter Lifesciences, a division of Danaher Corp.

A10 plans to pay Caron an annual salary of $400,000 with a signing bonus of $150,000. In 2024, Becker made a total compensation of $1.1 million, according to a separate filing.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Hundreds Arrested in Immigration Raid at Hyundai Site in Georgia — WSJ
data.description:
By Jiyoung Sohn, Ryan Felton and Timothy W. Martin

Nearly 500 people were arrested as part of an immigration raid at a Hyundai Motor battery plant under construction in Georgia as part of a criminal investigation into employment practices at the site, a Homeland Security official said Friday.

The operation Thursday, at an electric vehicle battery plant owned by Hyundai Motor and LG Energy Solutions, resulted in the arrest of 475 individuals. More than 300 were South Korean nationals, according to an official from the country.

Those arrested had illegally crossed the border, entered through a visa waiver program that prohibited them from working or had overstayed their visas, Steven Schrank, a special agent in charge of Homeland Security Investigations in Atlanta, said at a news conference Friday morning.

"This was the largest single site enforcement operation in the history of Homeland Security investigations," Schrank said.

No criminal charges were filed as of Friday, he said, and the investigation remains ongoing.

"Those who exploit our workforce, undermine our economy and violate our federal laws will be held accountable," Schrank said. He said the government's investigation has been going on for months.

The carmaker has pledged $26 billion in U.S. investments in recent weeks.

South Korea protested the action to the U.S. and said it was trying to secure the release of its citizens.

"This was not an immigration operation where agents went into the premises, rounded up folks and put them on buses," Schrank said. "This has been a multimonth criminal investigation where we have developed evidence, conducted interviews, gathered documents and presented that evidence to the court in order to obtain a judicial search warrant."

A search warrant in the case was issued Aug. 31, according to a court filing. The government filed a motion to unseal a redacted version of the warrant Friday, and a judge granted the request. A copy of the warrant wasn't immediately available.

"The United States is proud to be a home for major investments and looks forward to continuing to build on these historic investments and partnerships that President Trump has secured," said Abigail Jackson, a White House spokeswoman. "Any foreign workers brought in for specific projects must enter the United States legally and with proper work authorizations. President Trump will continue delivering on his promise to make the United States the best place in the world to do business, while also enforcing federal immigration laws."

Investigators are continuing to look at which company employed each of the individuals, Schrank said, but he noted a network of subcontractors worked at the site.

The raid and arrests come after months of tense negotiations between the U.S. and South Korea over tariffs and investment. In late July, the two countries agreed that a 15% tariff rate would be imposed on South Korean imports in exchange for South Korea investing $350 billion in the U.S.

The factory is being built by a joint venture between South Korea-based Hyundai Motor and a South Korean battery manufacturer, LG Energy Solution. The plant is located in the town of Ellabell, near Savannah, Ga., and is part of a $7.6 billion Hyundai complex that the state has described as the largest manufacturing project in Georgia's history.

The plant, once completed, is set to supply batteries to Hyundai Motor's nearby electric-vehicle manufacturing plant, which opened last year. Hyundai has pledged to employ 8,500 people there by 2031 as part of a $2 billion incentive package it received from Georgia.

Among those detained at the factory were South Korean employees of LG Energy Solution who were traveling for business. Hyundai Motor said it believed that it didn't directly employ any of those detained. LG Energy said Friday it was cooperating with the South Korean government and relevant authorities to ensure the employees' safety and secure their prompt release from detention.

The joint venture said it was cooperating with authorities and paused construction.

Michael Stewart, a spokesman for Hyundai, said the company is aware of the incident Thursday at the plant. "We are closely monitoring the situation and working to understand the specific circumstances," he said. "As of today, it is our understanding that none of those detained is directly employed by Hyundai Motor Company. We prioritize the safety and well-being of everyone working at the site and comply with all laws and regulations wherever we operate."

South Korea's Ministry of Foreign Affairs on Friday expressed regret at the detention of Korean nationals at the Georgia site and said it had conveyed its concerns to the U.S. Embassy.

"The economic activities of our investing companies and the rights and interests of our citizens must not be unjustly infringed during the process of U.S. law enforcement," the ministry said.

The abrupt arrest caught South Korean government officials and Hyundai by surprise. Seoul, a close U.S. ally, wasn't given prior notice by the Trump administration, according to a South Korean official.

The South Korean nationals working in Georgia were largely given visas suitable for training purposes, such as the B-1 visa, and many there were working as instructors, the South Korean official said.

Seoul expects an opportunity to affirm the legality of the visas held by the South Koreans working in Georgia.

News of the arrests jolted South Korea, whose president, Lee Jae Myung, had held a largely cordial summit with President Trump at the White House early last week.

South Korean companies in strategic industries including semiconductors, shipbuilding and batteries have pledged tens of billions of dollars in new investments in the U.S.

The Hyundai raid could raise concerns for South Korean companies that are sending Korean personnel to the U.S. and hiring locally for their plants, said Hur Jung, an economics professor who studies international trade at Sogang University in Seoul.

If such crackdowns are repeated, it "would damage trust and hurt industrial cooperation between the U.S. and South Korea across various industries, with negative repercussions for local communities as well," said Hur, who is also president of the Korean Association of Trade and Industry Studies.

In August, Hyundai Motor announced it would increase its U.S. investment to $26 billion through 2028, up $5 billion from its earlier pledge.

Several South Korean companies are investing in the U.S. Samsung Electronics is expanding chip-making facilities in Texas while SK Hynix is planning an advanced chip-packaging plant in Indiana. South Korean battery makers, including LG Energy Solution, Samsung SDI and SK On, have invested billions of dollars in building and operating U.S.-based factories across the Midwest.

South Korea is a partner in the Trump administration's push to "Make America Shipbuilding Great Again," and recently promised to spend $150 billion to help the U.S. revive its ship manufacturing, which has fallen far behind China.

After news of the Georgia arrests, South Korea's largest newspaper, the right-leaning Chosun Ilbo, ran a story with the headline, "After Investing in 'Trump MAGA,' What Came Back Was the Arrest of 300 Koreans.'

Write to Jiyoung Sohn at jiyoung.sohn@wsj.com, Ryan Felton at ryan.felton@wsj.com and Timothy W. Martin at Timothy.Martin@wsj.com

data.title: Victoria's Secret & Co. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Victoria's Secret & Co., a leading specialty retailer of women's intimate and other apparel, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial and operational performance, showcasing both growth and challenges faced during the period.

Financial Highlights

Net Sales: $1,459 million, increased 3% compared to the second quarter of 2024, driven by a 3% increase in North America stores and a 22% increase in international sales.

Gross Profit: $519 million, with a gross profit rate of 35.6%, up from 35.4% in the second quarter of 2024, primarily due to increased merchandise margin dollars.

Operating Income: $41 million, decreased by $21 million compared to the second quarter of 2024, with an operating income rate of 2.8% compared to 4.4% in the prior year, impacted by increased tariff and transportation costs.

Net Income Attributable to Victoria’s Secret & Co.: $16 million, down from $32 million in the second quarter of 2024, reflecting increased expenses and strategic shifts in marketing spend.

Net Income Per Basic Share Attributable to Victoria’s Secret & Co.: $0.20, compared to $0.41 in the second quarter of 2024.

Net Income Per Diluted Share Attributable to Victoria’s Secret & Co.: $0.20, compared to $0.40 in the second quarter of 2024.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The company's net sales in the second quarter of 2025 were driven by a 3% increase in the North American stores channel, a 5% decrease in the direct channel, and a 22% increase in the international channel. The international sales growth was attributed to increased sales in China, sourcing sales to partners, and royalties from franchise sales.

Geographical Performance: In North America, the stores channel saw a 3% increase in net sales, driven by higher average transaction values and conversion rates. The international channel experienced a significant 22% increase in net sales, with notable growth in China and other international markets.

Sales Units: The company reported a 6% increase in sales per average selling square foot and a 6% increase in sales per average store in the U.S. for the second quarter of 2025 compared to the same period in 2024.

New Product Launches: The company continues to focus on its strategic priorities, including the launch of new products in lifestyle categories such as sport and apparel, which contributed to the growth in average unit costs and inventory levels.

Future Outlook: The company remains committed to its strategic priorities, including supercharging its bras category, recommitting to the PINK brand, fueling growth in lifestyle categories, and evolving its brand projection and go-to-market strategy. The company is also focused on operational excellence, strategic capital allocation, and continued investment in differentiating capabilities to drive long-term profitable growth.

SEC Filing: Victoria's Secret & Co. [ VSCO ] - 10-Q - Sep. 05, 2025

Snowflake Inc., a leading provider of cloud-based data platforms, has released its latest Form 10-Q report, showcasing significant financial and operational achievements. The report highlights the company's robust revenue growth, improved cost management, and strategic business initiatives, reflecting its strong market position and future potential.

  • Revenue: $1,144.969 million, reflecting a significant increase from the previous year's $868.823 million, driven by strong demand for the company's cloud-based data platform.

  • Operating Loss: $(340.276) million, a slight improvement from the $(355.303) million loss in the previous year, suggesting efforts to control operating expenses.
  • Net Loss: $(297.930) million, compared to $(317.770) million in the prior year, showing a reduction in losses due to increased revenue and cost efficiencies.
  • Net Loss Per Share: $(0.89), an improvement from $(0.95) in the previous year, reflecting the company's efforts to enhance shareholder value despite ongoing challenges.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: The company reported product revenue of $1,090,496,000 and professional services and other revenue of $54,473,000 for the three months ended July 31, 2025. For the six months ended July 31, 2025, product revenue was $2,087,309,000 and professional services and other revenue was $99,734,000.
  • Geographical Performance: Revenue from the United States was $861,469,000 for the three months ended July 31, 2025, and $1,645,976,000 for the six months ended July 31, 2025. Other Americas contributed $30,570,000 and $60,231,000 for the three and six months ended July 31, 2025, respectively. EMEA region revenue was $185,315,000 for the three months and $354,124,000 for the six months ended July 31, 2025. Asia-Pacific and Japan generated $67,615,000 and $126,712,000 for the three and six months ended July 31, 2025, respectively.
  • New Product Launches: During the three months ended April 30, 2025, the company began marketing the Snowflake platform to selected public sector customers, allowing them to take possession of the company's software and contract with third parties to host it.

(the "Company"), Kinetik EC Holdco LLC, a Delaware limited liability company and indirect subsidiary of the Company ("Kinetik EC" and, together with Altus, the "Kinetik Sellers"), and, solely for the purposes set forth therein, Kinetik Holdings LP, a subsidiary of the Company, entered into a Purchase and Sale Agreement (the "EPIC Purchase Agreement") with Rattler Midstream Operating LLC, a Delaware limited liability company ("Rattler"), Rattler OMOG LLC, a Delaware limited liability company ("Rattler OMOG" together with Rattler, the "Diamondback Sellers" and, the Diamondback Sellers together with the Kinetik Sellers, the "Sellers"), Plains BK Holdco LLC, a Delaware limited liability company ("Buyer"), and, solely for the purposes set forth therein, each of Plains All American Pipeline, L.P., a Delaware limited partnership, and Rattler Midstream LP, a Delaware limited partnership, pursuant to which the Sellers have agreed to sell all of their respective partnership interests in EPIC Crude Holdings, LP ("EPIC"), collectively representing 55% of the outstanding interests in EPIC, to Buyer for a total purchase price of approximately $1.8 billion (the "EPIC Sale"), consisting of approximately $1.6 billion of cash (subject to customary adjustments, including deductions for outstanding indebtedness) to be paid at closing (the "Purchase Price") and an additional $192.5 million of contingent cash in the form of an earnout, payable upon the approval by the board of directors of the general partner of EPIC of one or more capital projects that achieve certain capacity expansion criteria (the "Earn Out"). data.title: Elon Musk Needs $1 Trillion in 'Motivation,' Says Tesla Board. The Stock Rises. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

Tesla stock jumped Friday as the company's board laid out plans to "motivate" CEO Elon Musk. Its proposal includes an unprecedented amount of motivation but investors appeared not to mind.

Shares of the electric-vehicle maker were up 4.4% at $353.55, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.3% and 0.1%, respectively.

Giving away a huge chunk of Tesla stock is what led investors to bid up shares. Tesla filed its 2025 proxy report, which includes details of Musk's 2025 performance incentive, which, if approved, would grant him about 425 million incentive-laden stock options. That is roughly equivalent to 12% of Tesla's stock outstanding. (He holds some 400 million shares of Tesla and controls another 300 million or so stock options).

The proxy includes some details about the process and, surprisingly, the language "if [Musk] were to remain at Tesla." That gives the impression it was possible he could move on. During negotiations, it was clear to the board that Musk was "motivated by all-or-nothing, pay-for-performance" and that he wanted 25% voting control of Tesla.

Pay for performance is fine, but 12% of a company's stock is a lot. Still, "the Special Committee believed it to be critical to Tesla to secure Mr. Musk's commitment and focus to lead Tesla."

All the options vest if Tesla achieves an $8.5 trillion market capitalization. That would put Tesla stock at, very roughly, $2,700 a share and make the 2025 performance award worth a cool $1 trillion. Musk's total Tesla stock, assuming no additional sales or legal issues, would be worth roughly $3 trillion.

The 2025 award follows a pattern for Tesla's board. In 2012, it awarded Musk about 80 million stock options. In 2018, Musk was awarded another 300 million-plus incentive-laden stock options.

It's an unprecedented amount of money for a CEO. The board believes it is getting strong management to guide the company through explosive growth. The awards "represent a comprehensive plan to retain and incentivize Mr. Musk to lead Tesla through a new period of transformative growth."

The board wants Musk focused on four things: cars, Tesla's full self-driving software, robo-taxis, and AI-trained robots.

Incentives beyond market capitalization are tied to those issues. Tesla would need to deliver 20 million vehicles, achieve 10 million active FSD subscriptions, deliver one million Optimus humanoid robots, and have one million robo-taxis in commercial operation. All that would amount to $400 billion in annual earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, or Ebitda, according to a target laid out in the proxy.

Apple is expected to generate about $137 billion in 2025 Ebitda, according to FactSet. Tesla is expected to generate about $13 billion.

The package "represents a critical next step to keep Musk as CEO at least until 2030," wrote Wedbush analyst Dan Ives in a report Friday. "Tesla is heading into one of the most important stages of its growth cycle with the autonomous and robotics future now on the doorstep."

Ives rates Tesla stock a Buy and has a $500 price target for the stock.

William Blair analyst Jed Dorsheimer rates shares Hold and doesn't have a price target for the stock. He thinks keeping Musk is a good idea, too.

"Tesla's new incentive plan for Elon Musk signals he is here to stay and win. If he can pull off the long-term goal of an $8.5 trillion market cap company," wrote Dorsheimer on Friday. "This would be an incredible achievement and aligned with shareholders." He sees the measure, to be voted on by shareholders, passing easily.

Tesla's stock move shows that investors align with those views. They don't care about huge pay packages as long as Musk is at the helm. It appears he will be, now that he has the appropriate motivation.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

data.title: Trump signs order to rename Department of Defense the 'Department of War'
data.description: U.S. President Donald Trump signed an executive order on Friday to rename the Department of Defense the "Department of War."

data.title: Trump orders return to the US 'War Department'
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump signed an executive order on Friday to rename the Department of Defense the "Department of War," reverting to a title it held until after World War Two when officials sought to emphasize the Pentagon's role in preventing conflict.

Trump signed the executive order at a ceremony in the Oval Office. It was the latest rebranding of the U.S. military and included his decision to preside over an extraordinary military parade in downtown Washington, D.C., and to restore the original names of military bases that were changed after racial justice protests in 2020.

He has also challenged conventional norms over domestic deployment of military, creating military zones along the southern U.S. border with Mexico to aid an immigration crackdown as well as deploying troops in cities like Los Angeles and Washington.

The order would authorize Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and subordinate officials to use secondary titles such as "Secretary of War" and "Deputy Secretary of War" in official correspondence and public communications, according to a White House fact sheet.

The move would instruct Hegseth to recommend legislative and executive actions required to make the renaming permanent.

Department name changes are rare and require congressional approval, but Trump's fellow Republicans hold slim majorities in both the Senate and House of Representatives, and the party's congressional leaders have shown little appetite for opposing any of Trump's initiatives.

Two Republican senators, Mike Lee of Utah and Rick Scott of Florida, and one Republican House member, Greg Steube of Florida, introduced legislation on Friday to make the change.

The U.S. Department of Defense was called the War Department until 1949, when Congress consolidated the Army, Navy and Air Force in the wake of World War Two. Historians say the name was chosen in part to signal that in the nuclear age, the U.S. was focused on preventing conflict.

Changing the name again will be costly and require updating signs and letterheads used not only by officials at the Pentagon in Washington, D.C., but also military installations around the world.

An effort by former President Joe Biden to rename nine bases that honored the Confederacy and Confederate leaders was set to cost the Army $39 million. That effort was reversed by Hegseth this year.

Critics have said the planned name change is not only costly, but an unnecessary distraction for the Pentagon.

Hegseth has said that changing the name is "not just about words — it's about the warrior ethos."

This year, one of Trump's closest congressional allies, Republican U.S. House of Representatives Oversight Committee Chair James Comer, introduced a bill that would make it easier for a president to reorganize and rename agencies.

"We're just going to do it. I'm sure Congress will go along if we need that ... Defense is too defensive. We want to be defensive, but we want to be offensive too if we have to be," Trump said last month.

Trump also mentioned the possibility of a name change in June, when he suggested that the name was originally changed to be "politically correct."

But for some in the Trump administration, the effort goes back much further.

During Trump's first term, current FBI Director Kash Patel, who was briefly at the Pentagon, had a sign-off on his emails that read: "Chief of Staff to the Secretary of Defense & the War Department."

"I view it as a tribute to the history and heritage of the Department of Defense," Patel told Reuters in 2021.

data.title: MillerKnoll Names Veltman Interim CFO, Stutz COO
data.description:
By Freddy Sebastian

Office-furniture company MillerKnoll named Kevin Veltman as interim chief financial officer, replacing Jeff Stutz, who was appointed chief operating officer.

Stutz has served as CFO since 2015 and helped conduct Herman Miller's acquisition of Knoll in 2020, the company said Friday.

MillerKnoll also tapped John Hoke as board chair, replacing Michael Volkema, who is planning to retire at the end of his term after serving on the board for 25 years. The move is effective Oct. 13.

Veltman is currently the company's senior vice president of finance and will serve as CFO on an interim basis while a search is underway for a permanent replacement.

The company is due to report first-quarter earnings on Sept. 23.

Write to Freddy Sebastian at freddy.sebastian@wsj.com

data.title: Grayscale Digital Large Cap Fund LLC SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Grayscale Digital Large Cap Fund LLC, a Cayman Islands limited liability company focused on holding top digital assets by market capitalization, has released its Form 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the evolving digital asset market.

Financial Highlights

Net realized and unrealized gain on investments in digital assets: $266,399 thousand, reflecting a realized gain of $12,995 thousand on the transfer of digital assets to pay the Manager’s Fee, a realized gain of $9,271 thousand as a result of the quarterly rebalance of digital assets, and net change in unrealized appreciation on investments in digital assets of $244,133 thousand.

Net increase in net assets resulting from operations: $250,266 thousand, which consisted of the net realized and unrealized gain on investments in digital assets, less the Manager’s Fee of $16,133 thousand.

Net assets: $777,222 thousand as of June 30, 2025, a 47% increase for the year, resulting from the price appreciation of Fund Components, partially offset by the withdrawal of digital assets to pay the Manager’s Fee.

Business Highlights

Fund Overview: The Grayscale Digital Large Cap Fund LLC is a Cayman Islands limited liability company formed to hold top digital assets by market capitalization. It issues shares to accredited investors and is quoted on OTCQX under the ticker symbol 'GDLC'.

Listing and Regulatory Status: The Fund's application to list on NYSE Arca was approved by the SEC but is currently under review, with a stay imposed on the order. The Fund is registered with the Cayman Islands Monetary Authority under the Private Funds Act.

Investment Objective: The Fund aims for the value of its shares to reflect the value of the digital assets it holds, based on their respective index prices and weightings, minus expenses and liabilities.

Index Transition: As of June 5, 2025, the Fund transitioned from using the CoinDesk Large Cap Select Index (DLCS) to the CoinDesk 5 Index (CD5) for determining its components, focusing on the largest and most liquid digital assets.

Rebalancing Strategy: The Fund rebalances its portfolio quarterly, adjusting its holdings based on changes in the index components and weightings. This process involves buying and selling digital assets to align with the index methodology.

Digital Asset Holdings: As of June 30, 2025, the Fund's digital asset holdings included Bitcoin, Ether, Solana, XRP, and Cardano, representing a significant portion of the digital asset market capitalization.

Custody and Security: The Fund's digital assets are secured by Coinbase Custody Trust Company, LLC, using cold storage mechanisms to protect private key shards, ensuring enhanced security and minimal credit risk.

Future Outlook: The Fund intends to seek listing on NYSE Arca and may operate a redemption program subject to regulatory approval. It also plans to continue adapting its portfolio to reflect changes in the digital asset market.

Strategic Initiatives

Strategic Initiatives: The Fund has transitioned from the DLCS Methodology to the CD5 Methodology as of June 5, 2025, to better align with market conditions and improve the valuation of its digital asset holdings. This strategic shift is aimed at enhancing the Fund's investment objective by reflecting the value of the Fund Components more accurately. Additionally, the Fund has periodically adjusted its portfolio by rebalancing and incorporating new digital assets such as ADA, XRP, and AVAX, in line with its investment methodologies.

Capital Management: The Fund has not engaged in traditional capital management activities such as debt issuance or equity repurchase due to its structure as a passive investment vehicle. However, it has managed its capital by issuing Shares in exchange for digital assets and adjusting its portfolio to align with its investment methodologies. The Fund's Shares have been quoted on OTCQX, and the price has varied significantly from the NAV per Share, trading at both premiums and discounts. The Fund has not accepted redemption requests as of June 30, 2025, and has maintained a zero cash balance, with expenses primarily covered by the Manager's Fee.

Future Outlook: The Fund plans to continue its strategic focus on aligning its portfolio with the CD5 Methodology, which may involve further adjustments to its digital asset holdings. The Fund aims to maintain its passive investment strategy, providing investors with exposure to digital assets without utilizing leverage or derivatives. The Fund will also continue to monitor market conditions and adjust its methodologies as necessary to ensure alignment with its investment objectives.

Challenges and Risks

Challenges and Risks: The company faces significant risks related to the digital asset market, including the potential for the SEC to classify some digital assets as securities, which could lead to adverse effects on asset value and fund termination. The extreme volatility of digital asset prices poses a risk of substantial value loss for shares. The evolving nature of blockchain technology and digital assets introduces uncertainties regarding their long-term value and acceptance. Regulatory changes in the U.S. and abroad could impact the value of shares and restrict digital asset use. The unregulated nature of digital asset trading platforms increases the risk of fraud, market manipulation, and operational failures, which could adversely affect asset values.

Management highlights the extreme volatility in digital asset prices, exacerbated by events such as the FTX collapse and regulatory actions against major trading platforms like Binance and Coinbase. These events have led to increased scrutiny and potential regulatory changes that could impact the digital asset market. The company does not actively manage the fund to mitigate volatility impacts, which could lead to significant value loss. The introduction of a U.S. government-funded strategic cryptocurrency reserve has also influenced market dynamics, affecting digital asset prices.

The company is exposed to market risks due to the high volatility and speculative nature of digital assets. The lack of regulation and transparency in digital asset trading platforms increases the risk of market manipulation and fraud. The potential for forks and clones in digital asset networks could lead to value fluctuations and operational challenges. The concentration of digital asset ownership and the influence of large holders could adversely affect market prices. Additionally, the company faces risks related to the environmental impact of digital asset mining and potential regulatory restrictions on mining activities.

data.title: Ibovespa Closes at Historic High
data.description:
The Ibovespa gained 1.2% to close at a record 142,639 on Friday, amid weakening US economic signals that reinforced expectations for multiple Fed rate cuts this year and by Brazil’s improving domestic backdrop.

Weak US payrolls triggered a drop in Treasury yields and renewed global liquidity flows into emerging markets, while easing inflation projections at home and new measures from Brazil’s central bank to enhance financial system security added layers of confidence in the macro-financial environment.

Gains were broadbased with major banks among top performers including Banco Do Brasil, Santander, Itaúsa, and Bradesco adding between 1% and 3.5%.

data.title: Weak Jobs Data Boosts Rate-Cut Bets; Dow Falls — WSJ
data.description:
By Chelsey Dulaney and Vicky Ge Huang

U.S. stocks ended modestly lower Friday after a weak jobs report cemented the case for a Federal Reserve rate cut at the central bank's policy meeting ending Sept. 17.

Bond yields declined sharply. Stocks opened higher in a bet that lower rates will boost corporate profits. But they turned lower at midmorning, reflecting concerns that rate cuts won't necessarily jumpstart economic growth. The Dow fell 0.5% and the S&P 500 was off 0.3%; the Nasdaq was a tick lower.

The nonfarm payrolls report showed the U.S. added just 22,000 jobs in August, undershooting forecasts, and contracted in June. For last month, the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.3%, matching forecasts.

Investors have already ramped up wagers that the Fed will resume cutting interest rates, helping push stocks to record highs. Traders moved early Friday to price in a quarter-point mid-September cut, with an outside chance of a half-point reduction.

President Trump has been pressuring Fed Chair Jerome Powell for months to cut rates. In a social-media post Friday, he said Powell "should have lowered rates long ago."

On Friday:

Kenvue stock fell 9% after The Wall Street Journal reported that Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. plans to announce that pregnant women's use of Tylenol, made by a Kenvue division, is potentially linked to autism.

Broadcom shares rose after upbeat quarterly results and news that OpenAI will make chips designed by it. Other chip stocks were mostly lower, led by a decline in AMD.

Tesla's board proposed as much as $1 trillion in pay for Elon Musk over a decade.

The European Union fined Google nearly $3.5 billion for abusing the dominance of its advertising-technology tools
